---
title: "Abstract Algebra Coding Project"
subtitle: "Symmetry, Structure, and the Geometry of Weight Space"
format:
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    papersize: letter
    geometry:
      - margin=0.85in
    fontsize: 11pt
    colorlinks: true
    number-sections: false
    header-includes: |
      \usepackage{booktabs}
      \usepackage{amssymb}
      \usepackage{amsmath}
      \usepackage{tikz}
      \usepackage{enumitem}
      \usetikzlibrary{arrows.meta, positioning}
      \setlist{nosep, leftmargin=1.5em}
      \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.35}
---

\begin{tabular}{@{}ll@{}}
\toprule
\textbf{Time estimate} & \~{}4 hours \\
\textbf{Languages} & C++ (group computation) + Python/PyTorch (network experiments) \\
\textbf{Output} & Group tables + orbit analysis + polynomial ring demo + symmetry landscape figure \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}

\vspace{1em}

Neural networks have hidden algebraic structure: permutation symmetries, sign symmetries, and polynomial descriptions of their loss landscapes. This project builds from group axioms through actions and representations to a concrete demonstration that algebra governs the geometry of optimization.

Each part builds on the last: you construct the symmetry group in Part 1, study how it acts on weight space in Part 2, connect to polynomial descriptions in Part 3, and see the consequences for the loss landscape in Part 4.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=Stealth, scale=0.8, every node/.style={font=\small}]
  \foreach \i in {1,2} {
    \node[draw, circle, minimum size=7mm] (i\i) at (0, -\i*1.2+1.8) {$x_\i$};
  }
  \foreach \j in {1,2,3} {
    \node[draw, circle, minimum size=7mm, fill=blue!10] (h\j) at (3, -\j*1.2+2.4) {$h_\j$};
  }
  \node[draw, circle, minimum size=7mm] (o) at (6, 0) {$y$};
  \foreach \i in {1,2} {
    \foreach \j in {1,2,3} {
      \draw[->] (i\i) -- (h\j);
    }
  }
  \foreach \j in {1,2,3} {
    \draw[->] (h\j) -- (o);
  }
  \node[below, font=\footnotesize] at (3, -2.2) {Permuting $h_1, h_2, h_3$: $S_3$ acts on weights. Flipping signs: $(\mathbb{Z}_2)^3$ also acts.};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

# Part 1 --- Group Tables and the Full Symmetry Group (C++, \~1h)

**Goal:** Build the symmetry group of a 2-3-1 ReLU network from scratch and verify the group axioms computationally.

## 1a. $S_3$ permutation matrices

Generate all 6 elements of $S_3$ as $3\times 3$ permutation matrices. Compute the full $6\times 6$ multiplication table: for each pair $(P_\sigma, P_\tau)$, compute $P_\sigma P_\tau$ and identify which element it equals.

Verify the group axioms:
1. \textbf{Closure:} Every product is in the table
2. \textbf{Associativity:} $(P_\sigma P_\tau) P_\rho = P_\sigma (P_\tau P_\rho)$ for 10 random triples
3. \textbf{Identity:} $I$ is in the group
4. \textbf{Inverses:} Each element has an inverse in the table

## 1b. $(\mathbb{Z}_2)^3$ sign symmetry

For a ReLU network, flipping the sign of all incoming \emph{and} outgoing weights of a hidden neuron preserves the function (because $\text{ReLU}(-(-x)) = \text{ReLU}(x)$ when the output weight also flips). This gives a $(\mathbb{Z}_2)^3$ symmetry.

Represent each of the $2^3 = 8$ sign-flip elements as a diagonal matrix $D = \text{diag}(\pm 1, \pm 1, \pm 1)$. The sign flip acts as:
$$W_1 \mapsto D\, W_1, \qquad W_2 \mapsto W_2\, D$$

Generate all 8 elements. Verify each preserves the network function on 10 random inputs.

## 1c. The full symmetry group $(\mathbb{Z}_2)^3 \rtimes S_3$

The full symmetry group combines permutations and sign flips: $|G| = 8 \times 6 = 48$ elements. Each element is a pair $(\text{sign vector}, \text{permutation})$.

Compute the multiplication rule: $(D_1, P_1) \cdot (D_2, P_2) = (D_1 \cdot P_1 D_2 P_1^{-1},\; P_1 P_2)$. (The permutation conjugates the sign flips --- this is the semidirect product.)

Generate all 48 elements. Print the group order and verify closure on 20 random pairs.

# Part 2 --- Orbits, Stabilizers, and the Quotient Space (Python, \~1h)

**Goal:** Study how the full symmetry group acts on weight space and verify the orbit-stabilizer theorem.

## 2a. Orbits under the full group

Initialize a random 2-3-1 network. Apply all 48 group elements to the weight vector. Cluster the results by functional equivalence (output difference $< 10^{-10}$ on test inputs).

- For a generic weight vector, expect $|\text{Orb}| = 48$, $|\text{Stab}| = 1$
- Verify: $|\text{Orb}| \times |\text{Stab}| = 48$

## 2b. Non-trivial stabilizers

Construct weight vectors with special structure:

| Configuration | Stabilizer | $|\text{Stab}|$ | $|\text{Orb}|$ |
|:-------------|:-----------|:--------|:--------|
| All neurons distinct | Trivial | 1 | 48 |
| Two identical neurons | $S_2$ (swap those two) | 2 | 24 |
| All three identical | $S_3$ (any permutation) | 6 | 8 |
| Two identical + one zero output | $S_2 \times \mathbb{Z}_2$ | 4 | 12 |

Verify the orbit-stabilizer theorem for each case.

## 2c. The quotient space $W / G$

The \textbf{true} parameter space is the quotient $W / G$: each orbit is a single point. Compute the effective number of distinct weight configurations by dividing the total volume by the average orbit size. For our 2-3-1 network with 13 parameters, the naive parameter count is 13, but the effective count is $13 - \log_2(48) \approx 7.4$. This is why parameter counting overestimates model complexity.

# Part 3 --- Polynomial Rings and the Loss Landscape (Python, \~1h)

**Goal:** See how polynomial algebra describes the critical points of neural network loss functions.

## 3a. The loss as a polynomial

For a 1-hidden-unit network $f(x; a, b) = a \cdot \text{ReLU}(bx)$ with quadratic loss on data $\{(x_i, y_i)\}$:
$$L(a, b) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n \bigl(y_i - a\,\text{ReLU}(bx_i)\bigr)^2$$

For $x > 0$, $\text{ReLU}(bx) = bx$, so $L(a,b) = \frac{1}{n}\sum (y_i - abx_i)^2$ --- a polynomial in the \textbf{ring} $\mathbb{R}[a, b]$.

1. Generate 50 data points from $y = 2x + \epsilon$, $x > 0$
2. Compute $L(a, b)$ on a fine grid and plot as a contour map
3. Find the critical points: $\nabla L = 0$. The set of global minima is the \textbf{variety} $V(\nabla L)$. For this model, it's the hyperbola $ab = 2$.
4. Plot the variety $ab = 2$ on the contour map. This is a \textbf{one-dimensional algebraic variety} in the polynomial ring $\mathbb{R}[a,b]$.

## 3b. Ideals and the variety structure

The set of all polynomials vanishing on the critical set forms an \textbf{ideal} $I = \langle \partial L / \partial a,\; \partial L / \partial b \rangle$. Compute $\partial L/\partial a$ and $\partial L/\partial b$ symbolically (they are polynomials in $a, b$). Print the generators of the ideal.

Note: the variety $V(I)$ includes both the global minima (the hyperbola $ab=2$) and the singular point at the origin $(0,0)$ where the Jacobian is degenerate. Mark both on your plot.

# Part 4 --- Symmetry Meets the Loss Landscape (Python, \~1h)

**Goal:** Visualize how group symmetry creates the structure of the loss landscape.

## 4a. Loss along symmetry directions

Train a 2-3-1 ReLU network on a simple regression task (e.g., $y = \sin(x)$, 100 points). Extract the trained weights $\mathbf{w}^*$.

For each of the 48 group elements $g$:

1. Compute $g \cdot \mathbf{w}^*$ (the symmetry-equivalent weight vector)
2. Interpolate: $\mathbf{w}(\alpha) = (1-\alpha)\mathbf{w}^* + \alpha\,(g \cdot \mathbf{w}^*)$ for $\alpha \in [0, 1]$
3. Evaluate the loss at 50 values of $\alpha$

## 4b. The symmetry landscape figure

Create a figure with two panels:

- **Left:** Overlay all 48 loss curves. For the identity element, the curve is flat (trivially). For non-identity elements, the loss rises to a barrier at $\alpha = 0.5$ then returns to the same minimum at $\alpha = 1$. The barrier height varies --- some symmetry partners are ``closer'' in weight space than others.

- **Right:** A histogram of barrier heights. The distribution of barriers reveals the geometry of the quotient space $W/G$: nearby symmetry copies have low barriers, distant copies have high barriers.

\textit{Save as} \texttt{symmetry\_loss\_landscape.png}\textit{.}

## 4c. Equivariance verification

Using PyTorch, verify that convolution is equivariant to translation:

1. Create a random 1D signal of length 32 and a \texttt{Conv1d} layer
2. Shift the input by 5 positions (circular shift)
3. Verify: \texttt{conv(shift(x)) == shift(conv(x))} to machine precision

This is the practical consequence of group theory: architectures that \emph{build in} the symmetry (like CNNs) don't waste capacity learning it.

# Deliverables

\begin{enumerate}
  \item C++ multiplication table for $S_3$ with axiom verification
  \item C++ sign-flip group generation with functional equivalence proof
  \item Full 48-element group $(\mathbb{Z}_2)^3 \rtimes S_3$ with closure check
  \item Orbit-stabilizer verification for 4 weight configurations (generic, 2-equal, 3-equal, degenerate)
  \item Loss landscape contour with critical variety $ab = 2$ and singular point marked
  \item \texttt{symmetry\_loss\_landscape.png} --- 48 interpolation curves + barrier histogram
\end{enumerate}
